From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Classification of music by tradition or style

A **music genre** is a conventional category that identifies some pieces of [music](/wiki/Music "Music") as belonging to a shared tradition or set of conventions.[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Genre](/wiki/Genre "Genre") is to be distinguished from [musical form](/wiki/Musical_form "Musical form") and **musical style**, although in practice these terms are sometimes used interchangeably.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Music can be divided into genres in numerous ways, sometimes broadly and with polarity, e.g., [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") as opposed to [art music](/wiki/Art_music "Art music") or [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), or, as another example, [religious music](/wiki/Religious_music "Religious music") and [secular music](/wiki/Secular_music "Secular music"). Often, however, classification draws on the proliferation of derivative subgenres, fusion genres, and [microgenres](/wiki/Microgenre "Microgenre") that has started to accrue, e.g., [screamo](/wiki/Screamo "Screamo"), [country pop](/wiki/Country_pop "Country pop"), and [mumble rap](/wiki/Mumble_rap "Mumble rap"), respectively. The artistic nature of music means that these classifications are often subjective and controversial, and some may overlap. As genres evolve, novel music is sometimes lumped into existing categories.

## Definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definitions")]

Douglass M. Green distinguishes between genre and [form](/wiki/Musical_form "Musical form") in his book *Form in Tonal Music*. He lists [madrigal](/wiki/Madrigal_(music) "Madrigal (music)"), [motet](/wiki/Motet "Motet"), [canzona](/wiki/Canzona "Canzona"), [ricercar](/wiki/Ricercar "Ricercar"), and dance as examples of genres from the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music") period. To further clarify the meaning of *genre*, Green writes about "[Beethoven's Op. 61](/wiki/Violin_Concerto_(Beethoven) "Violin Concerto (Beethoven)")" and "Mendelssohn's Op. 64 ". He explains that both are identical in genre and are [violin concertos](/wiki/Violin_concerto "Violin concerto") that have different forms. However, Mozart's Rondo for Piano, K. 511, and the *Agnus Dei* from his Mass, K. 317, are quite different in genre but happen to be similar in form."[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 1982, [Franco Fabbri](/wiki/Franco_Fabbri "Franco Fabbri") proposed a definition of the musical genre that is now considered to be normative:[[4]](#cite_note-Moore-4) "musical genre is a set of musical events (real or possible) whose course is governed by a definite set of socially accepted rules", where a musical event can be defined as "any type of activity performed around any type of event involving sound".[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

A music genre or subgenre may be defined by the [musical techniques](/wiki/Musical_technique "Musical technique"), the cultural context, and the content and spirit of the themes. Geographical origin is sometimes used to identify a music genre, though a single geographical category will often include a wide variety of subgenres. Timothy Laurie argues that, since the early 1980s, "genre has graduated from being a subset of popular music studies to being an almost ubiquitous framework for constituting and evaluating musical research objects".[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The term *genre* is generally defined similarly by many authors and musicologists, while the related term *style* has different interpretations and definitions. Some, like [Peter van der Merwe](/wiki/Peter_van_der_Merwe_(musicologist) "Peter van der Merwe (musicologist)"), treat the terms *genre* and *style* as the same, saying that *genre* should be defined as pieces of music that share a certain style or "basic musical language".[[7]](#cite_note-Pete-7) Others, such as Allan F. Moore, state that *genre* and *style* are two separate terms, and that secondary characteristics such as subject matter can also differentiate between genres.[[4]](#cite_note-Moore-4)

### Subtypes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Subtypes")]

"Fusion genre" redirects here. For fusion genres in literary and film works, see [Hybrid genre](/wiki/Hybrid_genre "Hybrid genre").

See also: [Category:Fusion music genres](/wiki/Category:Fusion_music_genres "Category:Fusion music genres")

A *[subgenre](/wiki/Subgenre "Subgenre")* is a [subordinate](/wiki/Hierarchy "Hierarchy") within a genre.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) In music terms, it is a subcategory of a musical genre that adopts its basic characteristics, but also has its own set of characteristics that clearly distinguish and set it apart within the genre. A subgenre is also sometimes referred to as a style within the genre.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The proliferation of popular music in the 20th century has led to over 1,200 definable subgenres of music.

A musical composition may be situated in the intersection of two or more genres, sharing characteristics of each [parent](/wiki/Superior_(hierarchy) "Superior (hierarchy)") genre, and therefore belong to each of them at the same time.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) Such subgenres are known as *fusion genres*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Examples of fusion genres include [jazz fusion](/wiki/Jazz_fusion "Jazz fusion"), which is a fusion of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") and rock music, and [country rock](/wiki/Country_rock "Country rock") which is a fusion of [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") and rock music.

A *[microgenre](/wiki/Microgenre "Microgenre")* is a niche genre,[[13]](#cite_note-13) as well as a subcategory within major genres or their subgenres.

## Categorization and emergence of new genres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Categorization and emergence of new genres")]

The [genealogy of musical genres](/wiki/Genealogy_of_musical_genres "Genealogy of musical genres") expresses, often in the form of a written chart. New genres of music can arise through the development of new styles of music; in addition to simply creating a new categorization. Although it is conceivable to create a musical style with no relation to existing genres, new styles usually appear under the influence of pre-existing genres.

Musicologists have sometimes classified music according to a trichotomous distinction such as Philip Tagg's "axiomatic triangle consisting of 'folk', 'art' and 'popular' musics".[[14]](#cite_note-Analysing_Popular_Music_1982,_p._41-14) He explains that each of these three is distinguishable from the others according to certain criteria.[[14]](#cite_note-Analysing_Popular_Music_1982,_p._41-14) Tagg maintains that popular music differs from art music through its mass distribution strategy as well as its non-written distribution modes which produces distinct production and consumption patterns between these musical categories.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Automatic recognition of genres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Automatic recognition of genres")]

Main articles: [Automatic content recognition](/wiki/Automatic_content_recognition "Automatic content recognition") and [Music information retrieval](/wiki/Music_information_retrieval "Music information retrieval")

Automatic methods of [musical similarity](/wiki/Musical_similarity "Musical similarity") detection, based on [data mining](/wiki/Data_mining "Data mining") and [co-occurrence](/wiki/Co-occurrence "Co-occurrence") analysis, have been developed to classify music titles for electronic music distribution.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Glenn McDonald, the employee of [The Echo Nest](/wiki/The_Echo_Nest "The Echo Nest"), music intelligence and data platform, owned by [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"), has created a categorical perception spectrum of genres and subgenres based on "an algorithmically generated, readability-adjusted scatter-plot of the musical genre-space, based on data tracked and analyzed for 5,315 genre-shaped distinctions by Spotify" called *[Every Noise at Once](/wiki/Every_Noise_at_Once "Every Noise at Once").*[[18]](#cite_note-guardian-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Alternative approaches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Alternative approaches")]

Alternatively, music can be assessed on the three dimensions of "arousal", "valence", and "depth".[[20]](#cite_note-ECON-20) Arousal reflects physiological processes such as stimulation and relaxation (intense, forceful, abrasive, thrilling vs. gentle, calming, mellow), valence reflects emotion and mood processes (fun, happy, lively, enthusiastic, joyful vs. depressing, sad), and depth reflects cognitive processes (intelligent, sophisticated, inspiring, complex, poetic, deep, emotional, thoughtful vs. party music, danceable).[[20]](#cite_note-ECON-20) These help explain why many people like similar songs from different traditionally segregated genres.[[20]](#cite_note-ECON-20)

Starting from the end of the 20th century, [Vincenzo Caporaletti](/wiki/Vincenzo_Caporaletti "Vincenzo Caporaletti") has proposed a more comprehensive distinction of music genres based on the "formative medium" with which a music is created, that is the creative interface (cognitive milieu) employed by the artist. Following this framework, formative media may belong to two different matrixes: visual or audiotactile with regards to the role played in the creative process by the visual rationality or the bodily sensitivity and embodied cognition. The theory developed by Caporaletti, named Audiotactile Music Theory, categorises music in three different branches: 1) written music, like the so-called classical music, that is created using the visual matrix; 2) oral music (like [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") or ethnic music before the advent of sound recording technologies); 3) Audiotactile music, which are process of production and transmission is pivoted around sound recording technologies (for example [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), pop, rock, [rap](/wiki/Rap "Rap") and so on). These last two branches are created by means of the above-mentioned audiotactile matrix in which the formative medium is the Audiotactile Principle.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Major music genres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Major music genres")]

### Art music (Classical Music)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Art music (Classical Music)")]

Main article: [Art music](/wiki/Art_music "Art music")

See also: [List of art music traditions](/wiki/List_of_art_music_traditions "List of art music traditions")

Art music primarily includes classical traditions, including both [contemporary](/wiki/Contemporary_classical_music "Contemporary classical music") and historical classical music forms. Art music exists in many parts of the world. It emphasizes formal styles that invite technical and detailed deconstruction[[23]](#cite_note-23) and criticism, and demand focused attention from the listener. In Western practice, art music is considered primarily a written musical tradition,[[24]](#cite_note-Arnold,_Denis-24) preserved in some form of [music notation](/wiki/Musical_notation "Musical notation") rather than being transmitted orally, by rote, or in recordings, as [popular](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") and [traditional music](/wiki/Traditional_music "Traditional music") usually are.[[24]](#cite_note-Arnold,_Denis-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Historically, most western art music has been written down using the standard forms of music notation that evolved in Europe, beginning well before the Renaissance and reaching its maturity in the Romantic period.

The identity of a "work" or "piece" of art music is usually defined by the notated version rather than by a particular performance and is primarily associated with the composer rather than the performer (though composers may leave performers with some opportunity for interpretation or improvisation). This is so particularly in the case of western classical music. Art music may include certain forms of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), though some feel that jazz is primarily a form of popular music. The 1960s saw a wave of avant-garde experimentation in free jazz, represented by artists such as [Ornette Coleman](/wiki/Ornette_Coleman "Ornette Coleman"), [Sun Ra](/wiki/Sun_Ra "Sun Ra"), [Albert Ayler](/wiki/Albert_Ayler "Albert Ayler"), [Archie Shepp](/wiki/Archie_Shepp "Archie Shepp") and [Don Cherry](/wiki/Don_Cherry_(trumpeter) "Don Cherry (trumpeter)").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Additionally, avant-garde rock artists such as [Frank Zappa](/wiki/Frank_Zappa "Frank Zappa"), [Captain Beefheart](/wiki/Captain_Beefheart "Captain Beefheart"), and [the Residents](/wiki/The_Residents "The Residents") released art music albums.

### Popular music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Popular music")]

Main article: [Popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music")

Popular music is any musical style accessible to the general public and disseminated by the mass media. Musicologist and popular music specialist Philip Tagg defined the notion in the light of sociocultural and economical aspects:

> Popular music, unlike art music, is (1) conceived for mass distribution to large and often socioculturally heterogeneous groups of listeners, (2) stored and distributed in non-written form, (3) only possible in an industrial monetary economy where it becomes a commodity and (4) in capitalist societies, subject to the laws of 'free' enterprise\_ it should ideally sell as much as possible.[[14]](#cite_note-Analysing_Popular_Music_1982,_p._41-14)

The distinction between classical and popular music has sometimes been blurred in marginal areas[[27]](#cite_note-27) such as [minimalist music](/wiki/Minimalist_music "Minimalist music") and light classics. Background music for films/movies often draws on both traditions. In this respect, music is like fiction, which likewise draws a distinction between [literary fiction](/wiki/Literary_fiction "Literary fiction") and [popular fiction](/wiki/Popular_fiction "Popular fiction") that is not always precise.

#### Country music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Country music")]

Main article: [Country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music")

Country music, also known as country and western (or simply country) and hillbilly music, is a genre of popular music that originated in the southern United States in the early 1920s. The origin of country music stems from European [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") as well as ballads and dance tunes brought by British immigrants who combined these elements with [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") and [spirituals](/wiki/Spirituals "Spirituals") of [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") to create a separate musical form.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

#### Electronic music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Electronic music")]

Main articles: [Electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") and [electronic dance music](/wiki/Electronic_dance_music "Electronic dance music")

Electronic music is music that employs [electronic musical instruments](/wiki/Electronic_musical_instrument "Electronic musical instrument"), digital instruments, or [circuitry-based](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics") [music technology](/wiki/Music_technology "Music technology") in its creation. Contemporary electronic music includes many varieties and ranges from experimental art music to popular forms such as electronic dance music (EDM).

#### Funk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Funk")]

Main article: [Funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk")

Funk is a music genre that originated in [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") communities in the mid-1960s when musicians created a [rhythmic](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm"), danceable new form of music through a mixture of soul, jazz, and rhythm and blues (R&B).

#### Hip-hop

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Hip-hop")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/DJ_Hypnotize_and_Baby_Cee.jpg/250px-DJ_Hypnotize_and_Baby_Cee.jpg)](/wiki/File:DJ_Hypnotize_and_Baby_Cee.jpg)

Two DJs practicing [turntablism](/wiki/Turntablism "Turntablism")

Main article: [Hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop")

Hip-hop or hip hop (originally known as disco rap, also as hip-hop music and sometimes rap music or rap) is a genre of music that emerged in the early 1970s alongside a hip-hop subculture built by the African-American and Latino communities of New York City. The musical style is characterized by the synthesis of a wide range of techniques, but rapping is frequent enough that it has nearly become a defining characteristic. Other key markers of the genre are the disc jockey (DJ), turntablism, scratching, beatboxing, and instrumental tracks. Cultural interchange has always been central to the hip-hop genre; it simultaneously borrows from its social environment while commenting on it. It can be broadly defined as a stylized rhythmic music that commonly accompanies [rapping](/wiki/Rapping "Rapping"),[[29]](#cite_note-29) a rhythmic and rhyming speech that is chanted.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

#### Jazz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Jazz")]

Main article: [Jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz")

Jazz is a music genre that originated in the African-American communities of New Orleans, Louisiana, United States, in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with its roots in blues and ragtime.

#### Latin music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Latin music")]

Main article: [Latin music](/wiki/Latin_music "Latin music")

Latin music is a genre of music that originated in the United States due to the growing influence of Latino Americans in the music industry. It is a term used by the music industry to describe music in a catch-all category for various music styles from [Ibero-America](/wiki/Ibero-America "Ibero-America").

#### Pop music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Pop music")]

Main article: [Pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music")

Pop is a genre of popular music that originated in its modern form during the mid-1950s in the United States and the United Kingdom. The terms popular music and pop music are often used interchangeably, although the former describes all music that is popular and includes many disparate styles.

#### Punk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Punk")]

Main article: [Punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock")

The aggressiveness of the musical and performative style, based on structural simplicity and the vigorous rhythms of rock'n'roll style, reinforced the challenging and provocative character, within the universe of modern music.

#### Reggae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Reggae")]

Main article: [Reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae")

Reggae music, originating from the late 1960s [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), is a genre of music that was originally used by Jamaicans to define themselves with their lifestyle and social aspects.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The meaning behind reggae songs tend to be about love, faith or a higher power, and freedom.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Reggae music is important to [Jamaican culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Jamaica "Culture of Jamaica") as it has been used as inspiration for many third world liberation movements. [Bob Marley](/wiki/Bob_Marley "Bob Marley"), an artist primarily known for reggae music, was honored by Zimbabwe's 1980 Independence celebration due to his music giving inspirations to freedom fighters. The music genre of reggae is known to incorporate stylistic techniques from [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"), [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), African, Caribbean, and other genres as well but what makes reggae unique are the vocals and lyrics.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The vocals tend to be sung in [Jamaican Patois](/wiki/Jamaican_Patois "Jamaican Patois"), [Jamaican English](/wiki/Jamaican_English "Jamaican English"), and [Iyaric](/wiki/Iyaric "Iyaric") dialects. The lyrics of reggae music usually tend to raise political awareness and on cultural perspectives.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

#### Rock music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Rock music")]

Main article: [Rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music")

Rock music is a broad genre of popular music that originated as "[rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll")" in the United States in the late 1940s and early 1950s, developing into a range of different styles in the mid-1960s and later, particularly in the United States and the United Kingdom.

#### Heavy Metal/Metal music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Heavy Metal/Metal music")]

Main article: [Heavy metal music](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music")

Heavy metal evolved from [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock"), [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock"), and [blues rock](/wiki/Blues_rock "Blues rock") in late 1960s and 1970s with notable acts such as [Black Sabbath](/wiki/Black_Sabbath "Black Sabbath"), [Judas Priest](/wiki/Judas_Priest "Judas Priest") and [Motörhead](/wiki/Mot%C3%B6rhead "Motörhead"). The popularity of heavy metal soared in the 1980s with bands such as [Iron Maiden](/wiki/Iron_Maiden "Iron Maiden"), [Metallica](/wiki/Metallica "Metallica") and [Guns 'n' Roses](/wiki/Guns_%27n%27_Roses "Guns 'n' Roses"). It has a rougher style and heavier sound than other forms of rock music, with notable subgenres such as [thrash metal](/wiki/Thrash_metal "Thrash metal"), [hair metal](/wiki/Hair_metal "Hair metal") and [death metal](/wiki/Death_metal "Death metal").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Soca

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Soca")]

Main article: [Soca music](/wiki/Soca_music "Soca music")

**Soca music**, or the "soul of calypso", is a genre of music that originated in [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago") in the 1970s. It is considered an offshoot of [calypso](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music"), with influences from [Afro-Trinidadian](/wiki/Afro%E2%80%93Trinidadians_and_Tobagonians "Afro–Trinidadians and Tobagonians") and [Indo-Trinidadian](/wiki/Indo%E2%80%93Trinidadians_and_Tobagonians "Indo–Trinidadians and Tobagonians") rhythms.[[34]](#cite_note-34) It was created by [Ras Shorty I](/wiki/Ras_Shorty_I "Ras Shorty I") (or Lord Shorty)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) in an effort to revive traditional calypso, the popularity of which had been declining amongst younger generations in Trinidad due to the rise in popularity of [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") from Jamaica and [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") and [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") from the United States. From the 1980s onward, soca has developed into a range of new styles.

#### Soul music and R&B

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Soul music and R&B")]

Main articles: [Soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") and [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues")

Soul music became a musical genre that came to include a wide variety of R&B-based music styles from the pop R&B acts at [Motown Records](/wiki/Motown_Records "Motown Records") in Detroit, such as [the Temptations](/wiki/The_Temptations "The Temptations"), [Gladys Knight & the Pips](/wiki/Gladys_Knight_%26_the_Pips "Gladys Knight & the Pips"), [Marvin Gaye](/wiki/Marvin_Gaye "Marvin Gaye") and [Four Tops](/wiki/Four_Tops "Four Tops"), to "[deep soul](/wiki/Deep_soul "Deep soul")" singers such as [Percy Sledge](/wiki/Percy_Sledge "Percy Sledge") and [James Carr](/wiki/James_Carr_(musician) "James Carr (musician)").[[37]](#cite_note-Motown_Museum-37)

#### Polka

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Polka")]

Main article: [Polka](/wiki/Polka "Polka")

The polka is originally a Czech dance and genre of dance music familiar throughout Europe and the Americas.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Religious music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Religious music")]

Main article: [Religious music](/wiki/Religious_music "Religious music")

Religious music (also referred as sacred music) is music performed or composed for religious use or through religious influence. [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), spiritual, and [Christian music](/wiki/Christian_music "Christian music") are examples of religious music.

### Traditional and folk music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Traditional and folk music")]

Main articles: [Folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and [contemporary folk music](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music")

[![A picture of a red and black button accordion](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Sonolite.jpg/250px-Sonolite.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sonolite.jpg)

[Piano accordion](/wiki/Piano_accordion "Piano accordion"): Italian instrument used in several cultures

Traditional and folk music are very similar categories, but it is widely accepted that traditional music encompasses folk music.[[39]](#cite_note-39) According to the [ICTM](/wiki/International_Council_for_Traditional_Music "International Council for Traditional Music") (International Council for Traditional Music), traditional music are songs and tunes that have been performed over a long period of time (usually several generations)**.** [[40]](#cite_note-40)

The [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") genre is classified as the music that is orally passed from one generation to another. Usually, the artist is unknown, and there are several versions of the same song.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The genre is transmitted by singing, listening and dancing to popular songs. This type of communication allows culture to transmit the styles (pitches and cadences) as well as the context it was developed.[[42]](#cite_note-:0-42)

Culturally transmitting folk songs maintain rich evidence about the period of history when they were created and the social class in which they developed.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Some examples of the Folk Genre can be seen in the [folk music of England](/wiki/English_folk_music "English folk music") and [Turkish folk music](/wiki/Turkish_folk_music "Turkish folk music"). English folk music has developed since the [medieval period](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and has been transmitted from that time until today. Similarly, Turkish folk music relates to all the civilizations that once passed thorough Turkey, thereby being a world reference since the east–west tensions during the [Early Modern Period](/wiki/Early_Modern_history "Early Modern history").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Traditional folk music usually refers to songs composed in the twentieth century, which tend to be written as universal truths and big issues of the time they were composed.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Artists including [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"); [Peter, Paul and Mary](/wiki/Peter,_Paul_and_Mary "Peter, Paul and Mary"); [James Taylor](/wiki/James_Taylor "James Taylor"); and [Leonard Cohen](/wiki/Leonard_Cohen "Leonard Cohen"), transformed folk music to what it is known today.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Newer composers such as [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran") (pop folk) and [the Lumineers](/wiki/The_Lumineers "The Lumineers") (American folk) are examples of contemporary folk music, which has been recorded and adapted to the new way of listening to music (online)—unlike the traditional way of orally transmitting music.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Each country in the world, in some cases each region, district and community, has its own folk music style. The sub-divisions of folk genre are developed by each place, cultural identity and history.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Because the music is developed in different places, many of the instruments are characteristic to location and population—but some are used everywhere: [button](/wiki/Button_accordion "Button accordion") or [piano accordion](/wiki/Piano_accordion "Piano accordion"), different types of flutes or trumpets, [banjo](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo"), and [ukulele](/wiki/Ukulele "Ukulele"). Both French and Scottish folk music use related instruments such as the [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle"), the harp and variations of [bagpipes](/wiki/Bagpipes "Bagpipes").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Social influences on music selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Social influences on music selection")]

Since music has become more easily accessible ([Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"), [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes"), YouTube, etc.), more people have begun listening to a broader and wider range of music styles.[[49]](#cite_note-:02-49) In addition, social identity also plays a large role in music preference. Personality is a key contributor for music selection. Those who consider themselves to be "rebels" will tend to choose heavier music styles like [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") or [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock"), while those who consider themselves to be more "relaxed" or "laid back" will tend to choose lighter music styles like [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") or classical music.[[49]](#cite_note-:02-49) According to one model, there are five main factors that exist that underlie music preferences that are genre-free,[*[contradictory](/wiki/Category:All_self-contradictory_articles "Category:All self-contradictory articles")*] and reflect emotional/affective responses.[[50]](#cite_note-:1-50) These five factors are:

1. A Mellow factor consisting of smooth and relaxing styles (jazz, classical, etc.).
2. An Urban factor defined largely by rhythmic and percussive music (rap, [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), etc.).
3. A Sophisticated factor ([operatic](/wiki/Opera_music "Opera music"), [world](/wiki/World_music "World music"), etc.)
4. An Intensity factor that is defined by forceful, loud, and energetic music (rock, metal, etc.).
5. A campestral factor, which refers to singer-songwriter genres and country.[[50]](#cite_note-:1-50)

### Individual and situational influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_genre&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Individual and situational influences")]

Studies have shown that while women prefer more treble oriented music, men prefer to listen to bass-heavy music. A preference for bass-heavy music is sometimes paired with [borderline](/wiki/Borderline_personality_disorder "Borderline personality disorder") and [antisocial](/wiki/Antisocial_personality_disorder "Antisocial personality disorder") personalities.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Age is another strong factor that contributes to musical preference. Evidence is available that shows that music preference can change as one gets older.[[52]](#cite_note-52) A Canadian study showed that adolescents show greater interest in pop music artists while adults and the elderly population prefer classic genres such as rock, opera, and [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz").[[53]](#cite_note-53)
